entry.title: Cannot create Custom Fields for Material Document Header in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are working on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition environment under SAP GROW, and we have encountered a limitation regarding Custom Fields at the Material Document Header level.

Our scenario:

We have a custom Fiori freestyle application that posts Material Documents via the I_MaterialDocumentTP RAP BO. In this application, the user enters an "Ingress ID" which is stored in the Reference Document / Material Document Header Text field (XBLNR / MaterialDocumentHeaderText). This Ingress ID is a key to a custom Z table that holds carrier/transport information (driver name, vehicle plate, tare weight, and gross weight). We need to display these four fields on an Adobe Form associated with the Material Document printout.

The problem:

To achieve this, we need to create four Custom Fields at the Material Document Header level so they can be populated during posting and later consumed by the Adobe Form. However, when we go to the Custom Fields app, the Business Context "Inventory Management: Material Document" (header level) is not available. We can only find "Inventory Management: Material Document Item", which only allows creating custom fields at the item level.

We also explored the available BAdIs:

  • MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA — This BAdI works at the item level. We can successfully enrich item-level custom fields, but it does not give us access to header fields like the Reference Document or Header Text.
  • MMIM_HEAD_PROVIDE_DATA — This BAdI works at the header level and provides access to header data. However, since we cannot create custom fields at the header level (due to the missing Business Context), there is no target field to write the transport information to.

What we need:

We need a way to create custom fields at the Material Document Header level, or an alternative approach that allows us to store four carrier-related fields (transport name, driver, plate number, tare weight) at the header level so they can be consumed by an Adobe Form during the Material Document printout.

Our questions:

  1. Is the Business Context "Inventory Management: Material Document" (header level) planned for a future release? If so, which release or timeline?
  2. Is there an alternative extensibility approach to add custom fields to the Material Document Header in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition while remaining Clean Core compliant?
  3. Has anyone faced a similar requirement and found a supported workaround?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Need to have a seperate flexible workflow for PO amen...
entry.content:
Dear @Sudharmv,

In Public Cloud this is not supported in standard configuration. For Purchase Orders SAP flexible workflow evaluates the active workflows in the defined order and ensures that only one matching workflow is started. In addition when an already approved PO is changed and ordered again SAP starts a new workflow instance based on the restart/start conditions. Because of this design you cannot create a separate PO amendment workflow with the same preconditions but a different approver chain purely through standard configuration

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/b5b82d3b98144ec789862fd63bc6112d.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3053155

If the business requirement is to route amended POs to different approvers the supported Public Cloud approach is to introduce a differentiator for the amendment case. SAP supports defining custom procurement preconditions in the Manage Workflow apps and it also supports custom workflow agent determination through Badi MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2. With that design, the amended PO can be identified separately and assigned to a different approval path

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002767845

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2646400

Recommended approach is keep the standard PO workflow with restart enabled for relevant PO changes and add either a custom precondition or custom agent determination so that the amendment case becomes distinguishable from the original PO. Without such a differentiator, standard configuration alone cannot deliver separate approvers for amendments when the preconditions remain the same

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
We really need to see the actual error message and any related stack traces.

During the dataserver creation an errorlog should be generated for the new dataserver ($SYBASE/$SYBASE_ASE/install/<dataserver_name>.log).

The file size should be relatively small so you should be able to upload it as an attachment (either edit your original question, or start a new 'Answer' to your question)

If the file has grown sufficiently large (eg, trying to build the dataserver several times) then consider renaming the file, running the install/build again, and then upload the new *.log file.

In the meantime, if you are running into the snap:validate stack trace issue (as alluded to by Bart), this has been addressed a couple times in this community (eg, this Q&A).  The workaround in that linked Q&A (assuming you're experiencing the same exact issue) does not require going to a newer version of the ASE product.  [As for why you were able to successfully install the product before ... *shrug* ... hard to say without knowing more about the old vs new environments; could be a difference in the OS/kernel, a different ASE PL## version, ????]

  2. provide an 'Answer' to your own question (ie, 'Comments' don't appear to allow file attachments); you should be able to 'Answer' your own question

entry.title: Re: Sales Order A2X OData V4 — Does a deep insert POST ro...
entry.content:
Dear @alexa1996,

For Sales Order (A2X) OData V4 in SAP_COM_0109 the safe standard position is that a single deep insert POST should be treated as all-or-nothing. SAP Public Cloud API documentation states that for deep insert requests the entities are processed sequentially and SAP documents Continue on Error only for the separate Sales Order Simulate (A2X) API, not for the real create API. Based on that standard design, if one item fails validation in the actual create request the request should be treated as failing as a whole, not as header saved with only valid items

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/7c1c960180ac43378c042e1cd4fe2f28.html

There is no standard configuration documented for Public Cloud that changes this create behavior to partial persistence. The documented standard approach is to use Sales Order Simulate (A2X) first review the returned messages, and only then send the real create request

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/215bd15c8d354eea9bb47f18838ab43d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/d4634b0c7f4f42698113ed362670c77f.html

Summary:

In standard Public Cloud deep insert create for Sales Order (A2X) OData V4 should be treated as transactional/all-or-nothing and there is no documented non-Badi option to save the header with only the valid items

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Access Control in ABAP CDS Using User Defined Aspects
entry.content:
This is really a valuable blog @NooruBohra   Just a add-on I feel will be good.

Developers often struggle when the data isn't filtering as expected. Adding a "How to Debug" section is extremely valuable:

  • SQL Trace (ST05): Show how to use ST05 to see the actual SQL statement generated. Developers can then see exactly how the  WHERE clause was expanded by the Aspect.

  • Authority Check Trace (STAUTHTRACE): Even though this is "User-Defined," the CDS access control still logs attempts here.

  • The NULL Pitfall: Mention that if the Aspect returns no values for a user, the user sees nothing by default. Providing a "fallback" or explaining how to handle "Super Users" who should see all data (using ASPECT User combined with PFCG) adds professional-grade complexity.

just adding it to be more informative.

entry.title: Re: Does PRIMARY KEY clustered check stored somewhere
entry.content:
@chris_keating, thank you.

 

I added the schema name and got the following query:

 

select
st.table_name as TabName
, st.clustered_index_id as ClusteredIdxID
, si.index_name as IdxName
, 'Primary Key' as IdxType
from
sys.systab st join sys.sysidx si join sys.sysuser u
where
si.index_category = 1 -- primary key
AND st.table_name = 'leagues'
AND st.creator = u.user_id
AND u.user_name = 'dba'

 

I will mark it as resolved.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Quality Management For Services In SAP Public Cloud 2...
entry.content:
Dear @shafiyashuquran,

Standard QM inspection lots are not available for pure service procurement / service entry sheet processing. SAP documented QM inspection-lot process is tied to materials and logistics events such as goods receipt, receipt from production / during production, posting to inspection stock and outbound delivery. SAP documented standard process for services by contrast, is lean service procurement with Service Entry Sheets, optional automatic SES creation from external timesheets, approval workflow and then invoice verification. There is no SAP Public Cloud standard process documented for automatically or manually creating a QM inspection lot for a service product / SES

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/fb5964360b9945998ac478b606787fc1.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/2d4bc6cb411044368a51a2bce739c8ad.html

If the business requirement is to control service quality before payment the supported Public Cloud design is to use SES approval workflow as the quality gate with service report attachments, acceptance/rejection and invoice posting only after approved service entry. If the requirement is specifically to have a real inspection lot then the inspected object must be modeled as a material received through a material logistics process because that is the inspection-lot model SAP documents in standard QM

Summary:

In standard Public cloud there is no QM inspection-lot process for pure services / service products. The standard alternative is Service Entry Sheet + approval workflow + invoice verification.

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: LGM Knowledge Snippet: From Pickup Schedule to Picku/Delivery document
entry.content:
If you operate a shipping process with regular carrier pickups, Pickup Schedules are your key tool for automating Pickup/Delivery Document (PDD) creation in SAP Logistics Management. This post walks through the end-to-end flow: from defining a schedule to having a PDD ready for execution.

What is a Pickup Schedule?

A Pickup Schedule captures the recurring pattern of a carrier's vehicle arriving at your shipping point. Instead of manually creating a Pickup/Delivery Document every time a truck is expected, you define the pattern once and the system generates PDDs automatically.

Each Pickup Schedule contains:

  • Carrier - which carrier operates the pickup
  • Shipping Point and Location - where the pickup takes place
  • Recurrence Pattern - which days of the week the pickup occurs (Monday through Sunday)
  • Arrival Times - when the carrier's vehicle is expected (stored in the location's local timezone)
  • Validity Period - when the schedule is active, optionally open-ended

For example: "CarrierParcel Carrier 4711 arrives every Monday and Thursday at 09:00 and 15:00 at Shipping Point 1011  valid from from now with open end." would look like this:

You maintain Pickup Schedules in the Maintain Pickup Schedules app.

From Schedule to PDD: The Synchronization

Pickup Schedules don't create PDDs on their own (so far ;.-) ) - a synchronization process reads the schedules and generates the corresponding PDDs.  This is done using the "Create Pickup Documents" button and selecting the time frame you want to create PDDs for, e.g. for next 14 days of for a defined time frame:

During synchronization, the system:

  • Reads all Pickup Schedules valid for the sync period
  • For each valid day and arrival time, creates a PDD  (if one doesn't already exist)
  • Populates the PDD with carrier, location, and planned arrival time from the schedule
  • Removes any previously created PDDs that no longer match the schedule (only if still empty and unused)

One important note: If a existing Pickup document for a given departure has been deleted manually, it will not be recreated for this dat/time combination. The ratio here is, that this pickup has been specifically cancelled and a recreate would contradicts the users intention. From that you can see, PDDs are not really deleted but stored as cancelled and basically hidden from any UIs, but still there for e.g. prevention of creation a duplicate for the cancelled date/time.  

The result: a set of empty PDD shells - headers with carrier and timing information, but no items yet. The items come later.

Timezone Handling

An important detail: arrival times in Pickup Schedules are stored in the shipping point's local timezone. During synchronization, these times are converted to UTC for the PDD's planned arrival time. This means a 14:00 pickup in Hamburg (CET) and a 14:00 pickup in New York (EST) will produce PDDs with different UTC timestamps, and each PDD correctly reflects the local expectation at the respective warehouse.

Filling the PDD: Transportation Request Assignment

With PDD in place, the next step is connecting them to actual goods. This happens through Transportation Request (TR) assignment, and the timing depends on your Processing Variant configuration. And some more info on that linkage can be found here. 

In a typical LTL or Parcel shipping process, the flow is:

  1. Transportation Requests are created and go through warehouse processing (picking, packing, staging)
  2. Once warehouse processing completes, the system searches for a matching PDD - one with the right carrier, location, and time window
  3. If a match is found, PDD items are created and linked to the TR items
  4. The TR's pickup planning status moves to Completed

This "late linking" pattern works well when you have regular carrier pickups and the warehouse prepares goods independently. The goods are assigned to the next suitable pickup slot as they become ready.

When No PDD is Found

If the system cannot find a matching PDD for a completed Transportation Request, the TR's pickup planning status changes to Error and is flagged with Action Required. This typically means:

  • No Pickup Schedule covers the required carrier/location/time combination
  • The schedule exists but the sync hasn't run yet for the relevant dates
  • The PDD was manually deleted or is no longer valid

To resolve this, create a PDD manually or update the Pickup Schedule and trigger a sync, then use the Retry PDD Assignment action on the Transportation Request.

The Complete Flow at a Glance

Putting it all together, here's the end-to-end journey from Pickup Schedule to loaded truck:

  1. Define - Maintain Pickup Schedules with carrier, location, days, and times
  2. Generate - Sync job creates empty PDD headers for upcoming pickup slots
  3. Prepare - Warehouse processes Transportation Requests (picking, packing, staging)
  4. Assign - Completed TRs are matched to suitable PDDs; PDD items are created
  5. Execute - Warehouse staff uses the PDD to know exactly what to load and when

Each step is automated through configuration, requiring no manual intervention in the standard case.

Practical Tips

  • Keep schedules current - If a carrier changes their pickup days or times, update the schedule and re-sync. Outdated schedules lead to unused PDDs or assignment errors.
  • Use manual sync after changes - After editing a Pickup Schedule, trigger a manual sync for the affected date range to immediately see the updated PDDs.
  • Monitor assignment errors - Regularly check for Transportation Requests in Error status. They often indicate gaps in your Pickup Schedule coverage.
  • Holiday handling - For days when no pickup occurs (holidays, closures), either adjust the schedule validity or delete the generated PDD. Deleted PDDs won't be regenerated by future syncs.

When Pickup Schedules Don't Apply

Not every shipping process uses Pickup Schedules. In a Direct LTL and the FTL process, the carrier is determined  on a per-consignment basis. Here, the PDD is created automatically when the carrier confirms acceptance - no schedule needed. The system creates the PDD with items already linked, and the pickup planning status is set to Not Relevant since late linking doesn't apply.

Related Topics

  • The Pickup/Delivery Document: Role and Creation Paths
  • Processing Variants: The Idea Behind Configuration
  • Configure Shipping Processes - SAP Help Portal
  • Maintain Pickup Schedules - SAP Help Portal

Questions about Pickup Schedules or the PDD assignment flow? Drop them in the comments!

entry.title: ILM Data Retrieval De-Archiving Options in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hello,

We would like to clarify the available options in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition regarding the possibility of restoring or de-archiving data managed through Information Lifecycle Management (ILM).

Specifically, we would like to understand whether it is possible to recover archived data back into the active system in exceptional scenarios, such as audit requirements or cases where data has been archived unintentionally.

 

Kind regards.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: ILM Data Retrieval De-Archiving Options in SAP S/4HAN...
entry.content:
Dear Custromer, 

 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does not support restoration or de-archiving of ILM-managed data back into the active system.

The system architecture is designed for one-way data movement: active database > archive files > eventual destruction.

For unintentional archiving, prevention is critical. Implement robust testing and approval processes to prevent unintentional archiving, as recovery is not guaranteed.

For audits, "restoring" the data is generally unnecessary because archived data remains readable, you can provide auditors with read-only access to archived data via Archive Explorer app.

For more information check:

  • SAP Help Portal: Information Lifecycle Management
  • SAP Best Practices Explorer: Scope Item 1KA - Information Lifecycle Management 

 

Kind regards,

SAP.

entry.title: Celebrating my 300th blog post on the SAP Community
entry.content:
I noticed that my last blog post was my 300th on the SAP Community. In those 300 posts, I have covered many topics related to user experience, clustered into a few key areas. To celebrate this milestone, I’ll reflect on how the topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP, what I learned from the community, what’s next, and end with a call to action for you.

My 300 blogs posts contain a historical account on the evolution of SAP’s user experience

Product Marketing is much easier with great products

Before going into the content, let me acknowledge that there is no product marketing without product. All these blog posts about our UX products would not have happened without the amazing development, product management, design, QA, documentation, and other colleagues that build and test these solutions. I’m grateful to the many coworkers over the years who came up with ideas to solve real customer pain points regarding SAP’s user experience. I also appreciate the many customers and partners that provided ideas for blogs posts in online forums, SAP events, and user groups.

The topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP

In my product marketing role, most of my posts fell into one of these broad categories:

  • Product
  • Strategy
  • Events
  • Customer and partner enablement

The topic mix shifted over time as our team expanded the products we worked on.

Product

  • Release announcements.
    Product-focused posts generally followed the cadence of our release cycles. This shifted from annual or semi-annual service packs for on-prem solutions to continuous innovation / continuous delivery pipelines for our cloud-based tools. My first five years of posts were on SAP Screen Personas. From version 1.0’s first appearance at TechEd 2012 through the release announcements of version 3 SP18, after which Rohith Deraje took over for SP19 and beyond.
    SAP Fiori elements and SAP Fiori tools were next to join our team’s portfolio. I posted some of the early announcements for those products until Katrin Polloczek, Christoph Gollmick, Ashley Tung, and Ian Quigley took ownership of the content.
    Collaborative ERP was a more recent addition to SAP UX Foundation. I posted the early announcements on our strategy and early versions until Regina Sheynblat picked this up.
    Embedded AI in SAP Fiori apps is the newest part of our portfolio, with Conrad Bernal now posting about new innovations and beta opportunities.
  • Roadmap updates – when you have a product that 1000s of customers use to run their business, they want to know what’s coming next. Towards that end, I drafted many roadmap updates over the years on the various solutions in our portfolio.
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQ) documents – one of the most interesting parts of my role is interacting with customers in various forums and hearing their questions. Often, we hear the same questions from multiple people. In those cases, it’s worth capturing them into an FAQ document.
  • Technical tips and tricks – as part of the product team, many customers reach out vie email to ask questions that are more detailed than can be covered in an FAQ document. If their inquiry is relevant to a broader audience, I put together tips they can use to get started on a project or break through a barrier they are facing and post it so others in the SAP Community can learn.
  • Customer and partner success stories – sometimes our customers want to share their success with SAP UX solutions. In those cases, I have helped them tell their story through the SAP Community.

Strategy

When I looked through my blog posts over time, they followed the same evolution of SAP’s broader UX strategy. We built SAP Screen Personas to improve the SAP GUI user experience, one screen at a time. SAP Fiori elements accomplished this at scale, for the most common business processes. Then we rolled out SAP Fiori tools to make it easier to build Fiori elements apps. Then collaborative ERP to help people team up to solve complex business problems. Most recently, we added AI to both the Fiori apps that we ship and the tools we (and you) use to build them.

Posts on UX strategy explain how all the pieces fit together so customers can plan their digital transformation, SAP S/4HANA migration, or other strategic initiative knowing where SAP is heading. Since customers are not concerned about our internal organizational structures, I blogged on solutions within the UX Foundation portfolio as well as adjacent solutions such as SAP Build, BTP, and Joule. Sometimes we include a peek behind the curtain, so people understand how SAP Fiori powers the apps in SAP S/4HANA and what to expect as we continue to add both simplicity and power to our flagship SAP Cloud ERP solution.

Events

It’s natural to blog about events to help people set the right expectations, plan their agendas, and make the most of their investment in attending the event. Typically, I like to highlight the sessions most relevant to the customers and partners that follow me on the SAP Community and LinkedIn.

For Sapphire 2026, I have a list of recommended sessions on AI and UX, along with what I’m planning with Nicole Smythe on bridging the skills gap to accelerate your AI projects.

My other posts have covered SAP TechEd, ASUG Tech Connect, ASUG chapter meetings, and, of course, our own SAP UX Innovation Day events. Next one is on June 11 in Palo Alto, if you are interested in attending.

Customer and partner enablement

I also noticed a number of blog posts on various training courses for customers, partners, and colleagues. In the early days, we created several openSAP training courses. The ones that are still relevant have moved to learning.sap.com. We also have posts on hands-on exercises from other events or tutorials from our development experts. It makes sense that people appreciate training from the team that builds and supports the products.

The SAP community is for learning, connecting, and listening

I would be remiss if I did not reflect on the SAP Community and the role it played in my personal growth at SAP. I learned so much from the questions and comments that people submitted, and through my interactions with the highly professional SAP Community team.

Much more than the number of blog posts I have written, I’m most proud of how many other colleagues I inspired to start blogging; that list continues to grow.

And thanks to my long-time friend, former colleague, and co-author, Michael Procopio, for getting me started on my blogging journey back in our days at HP Software.

The next UI will build from our Fiori experience and use AI heavily

While SAP Fiori solves many issues, we are still working to simplify how customers consume SAP and developers create apps. Needless to say, AI will play an increasingly important role in both of these dimensions. Your ongoing feedback will continue to drive our development priorities.
You will hear more about this at Sapphire and in our next SAP UX Innovation Day.

In addition to providing your feedback through the SAP Community, you are welcome to join our monthly SAP Developer Roundtable to share your thoughts on developing SAP Fiori apps using our latest tools and technologies.
If you are a public cloud customer, you can join our online user group to discuss UX and adjacent topics with your peers and SAP experts.

 

We will continue to address your concerns about usability in SAP Cloud ERP.

To count to 300, start with 1: What are you waiting for?

A much bigger milestone than blog 300 is blog #1.
What are you waiting for? There’s no time like the present!
The SAP Community needs your voice. If you want to brainstorm ideas or need help outlining your first post, please let me know. Like the rest of the SAP Community team, I’m here to help.

Thanks for reading all my stuff over the years.

entry.title: Preparing for the Interview
entry.content:
As we’ve seen in our last blog, candidate fraud can be a serious issue in the hiring process, compromising the integrity of your recruitment efforts. Now that you know how some SAP Fieldglass features can help you in the candidate selection process, in this blog, we’ll dive into features that can help you during the interview process. 

Hiring the right candidate can significantly impact the success and dynamics of your project. However, resumes and applications don't always tell the whole story. Before you even contact a candidate, there are several proactive techniques you can employ to recognize red flags: 

🚩Running initial checks on applicants, such as background screenings and reference confirmations 

🚩Using video chat tools to verify the identity of candidates before setting up interviews 

🚩Training the hiring team on typical scam methods and warning signs 

🚩Using safe communication methods to talk with candidates 

🚩Avoiding personal e-mail addresses or unprotected messaging apps 

Background Check Information 

With the Background Check Integration feature, you can add a background check to the SAP Fieldglass hiring process. It's a process in which info is sent and received between SAP Fieldglass and a selected background check provider. This setup guarantees that a supplier can't accept a work order until the background check is complete and passed. Once this feature is on, all candidates can have a background check. Here's the general process: 

  1. The supplier starts the background check and is redirected to the provider's website. 
  2. The supplier fills in the required info for the job seeker, and the background check provider does the check.  
  3. Once done, the results are posted to the SAP Fieldglass app. 

You can choose to skip the background check for individual work orders. For more information about the setup, see Background Check Integration.  

Here’s an example of an optional setup for the Job Seeker module: 

  1. Go to Admin Menu 
  2. Select Messaging under Messaging  
  3. Select Job Seeker under Module 
  4. Select the Background Check you want under Action 

Microsoft Office 365 Calendar Configuration  

The Microsoft Office 365 Calendar Integration is another feature that can help you before your interviews with job seekers. Integrating Microsoft Office 365 Calendar with SAP Fieldglass can help mitigate potential candidate fraud in several ways.  

Calendar invites instantly separate serious candidates from fake ones. Genuine applicants accept and confirm, while the others ghost. Real-time interviewer availability lets you schedule quickly, reducing the window for fraud candidates to slip through. Integrated calendars eliminate double-booking, so candidates get the attention they deserve while your team stays organized. Automated reminders keep everyone on track and flag potential no-shows. For remote teams, synchronized time zones eliminate confusion and miscommunication. Plus, every scheduled event creates an audit trail you can reference if suspicious activity arises. It's streamlined, secure, and very effective.  

To use this feature: 

  • Make sure that the integration between SAP Fieldglass and Office 365 Calendar is correctly set up. For this, see Microsoft Office Calendar Configuration to check the configuration steps. 
  • Regularly monitor and audit calendar activities, looking out for any unusual patterns or discrepancies. 

SAP Fieldglass and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Integration 

Integrating SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central with SAP Fieldglass can also help to mitigate fraud risks in your selection process. This integration creates a powerful fraud detection system that protects your recruitment process. By connecting these platforms, you gain real-time access to comprehensive employee data that enables you to verify candidate identities against existing records and instantly flag inconsistencies or duplicate applications under different names. The system automatically validates compliance requirements, such as work permits and visa status, ensuring candidates are legally eligible before interviews begin. 

Beyond basic verification, the integration establishes centralized access controls that allow only authorized users to view or modify candidate information, while maintaining a complete audit trail of all recruitment activities. This transparency makes it easy to spot unusual patterns or suspicious behavior. Best of all, you can configure intelligent risk scoring models that automatically evaluate candidates based on multiple data points, automatically detecting high-risk profiles for deeper investigation. 

To make the most of these integrations, ensure that both systems are properly configured. See the SAP Fieldglass and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Integration for more details.

How about checking some SAP Fieldglass features that can help you during your candidate selection phase? You can use them to create accountability and reduce fraudulent applications. 

Next, we’ll see what measures you can take during the actual hiring process that can help you to increase security when hiring candidates. Stay tuned! 

 

 

For the purposes of this guide, “candidate fraud” means deliberate misrepresentation of identity, credentials, work history, or qualifications during recruitment. This guide is preventive and educational; it does not make legal determinations.

entry.title: Re: GitHub Copilot for SAP ABAP in VS Code: Setup Guide for On-Premise Teams
entry.content:
Hi @shaikferoz   I did some research and I feel this is prerequisite to achieve. Please correct me if I am wrong. I want to try this.

To achieve this, we need to have some pre-requite Even with SAP GUI 800, you can achieve this setup. The key point is that the GitHub Copilot for ABAP workflow does not depend on the SAP GUI as a development environment. Instead, it relies on the ADT (ABAP Development Tools) communication layer exposed by your SAP backend.

With this, while the approach is valid, there are a few important prerequisites and practical points to ensure success in an on-premises ECC landscape.

1. Foundation Check (Critical Prerequisite)

Before starting, confirm your backend supports ADT:

  • Recommended: SAP NetWeaver 7.40+ (EHP7 or higher)
  • Ensure:
    • ADT services are available
    • Required authorizations are in place
    • HTTPS/ICF access is enabled

Note, Without ADT compatibility, this setup will not work regardless of GUI version. This is first major requirement to meet.

2. Activate ADT Services

Visual Studio (VS) Code communicates with SAP using ADT services (same as Eclipse).

Steps:

  • Open SAP GUI (800)
  • Go to transaction SICF
  • Navigate to: “/sap/bc/adt “if this folder is not there you can create. Sicf
  • Activate the service (if not already active)

Also verify:

  1. User has proper ICF and ADT authorizations
  2. Service is accessible via browser (test URL if needed)    

3. Now set up the Bridge: VS Code + ABAP Integration

Installation:

  • Visual Studio Code
  • ABAP Remote FileSystem

This extension allows you to browse and edit ABAP objects directly from VS Code by connecting to ADT services.

Note:

  • This is community-supported (not SAP official)
  • Works well for development, but not a full replacement for Eclipse ADT

4. Connection Setup (On-Premise)

In VS Code:

  • Open Command Palette → ABAP FS: Connection Manager
  • Add new connection:
    • Find port via SMICM → Services
    • URL: http(s)://<host>:<port>
    • Client: e.g., 110
    • Credentials: SAP user/password

In enterprise environments, also consider:

  • VPN / firewall access
  • SSL certificates
  • SSO/SNC configuration

5. Add AI Capability (Copilot)

Installation:

  • GitHub Copilot extension in VS Code

Once ABAP objects are opened via ABAP FS, Copilot can read and assist with code.

Important Tip (Highly Recommended)

For ECC systems, guide Copilot behavior:

Create:

.github/copilot-instructions.md

Add:

We are working on an SAP ECC system. Use traditional ABAP syntax.
Avoid 7.40+ inline declarations unless explicitly requested. This significantly improves output relevance.

 

6. Development Workflow (Hybrid Model)

You will follow a dual-tool workflow:

In VS Code:

  • Edit ABAP code
  • Use Copilot for:
    • Autocomplete
    • Code explanation
    • Refactoring suggestions

In SAP GUI:

  • Execute programs (SE38, transactions)
  • Handle:
    • Debugging
    • Transports (SE09/SE10)
    • Functional testing

Saving in VS Code writes directly to the backend system.

 

7. Limitations to Be Aware Of

This setup is powerful but not complete:

  • No full debugging support in VS Code
  • Limited refactoring vs Eclipse ADT
  • Performance may vary on older ECC systems
  • Copilot may suggest modern syntax not supported in your system

 

8. Why This Approach Still Adds Value

Compared to SE80:

  • Faster development with AI-assisted coding
  • Better readability and navigation
  • Ability to understand legacy ABAP quickly
  • Supports gradual modernization mindset

9. Recommended Approach (Do This First)

Instead of full rollout:

Run a pilot with 1–2 developers

Validate:

  • ADT connectivity
  • Object editing from VS Code
  • Copilot usefulness in real scenarios
  • Network/security constraints

Final Summary

You do not need to upgrade SAP GUI or move to cloud to use Copilot with ABAP.

However, success depends on:

  • ADT availability in your backend
  • Proper system configuration
  • Accepting a hybrid development model (VS Code + GUI)

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026: The Skills-Based Organisation Finally Grows Up
entry.content:
Two years ago, when I wrote about the 1H 2024 release of the Talent Intelligence Hub, I described TIH as the foundation of a skills-based organisation. At that point, much of the conversation was still aspirational — organisations were asking whether skills belonged at the centre of their HR strategy, and how the SuccessFactors suite would connect jobs, skills and people in one consistent model.

The 1H 2026 release is a different kind of moment. This is not a release of bright, shiny new toys. This is a maturation release — the point at which  SAP SuccessFactors is telling customers, very clearly, that the skills-based organisation is no longer a strategic aspiration. It is now the operational architecture of the suite, and the release makes three things unmistakable:

  1. Job Profile Builder (JPB) plus the Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is the future. Job Description Manager is being retired.
  2. Governance, sources of truth and trust have become first-class citizens. Skills data is finally being treated with the discipline HR data has always deserved.
  3. Career mobility, development and succession are being re-platformed on top of this foundation, with Joule agents doing some of the heaviest lifting.

If you are running SuccessFactors at enterprise scale — and especially if you have been putting off hard conversations about your job architecture or your skills governance — this release is going to force those conversations. In this blog, I'll walk through the updates that genuinely matter for leaders working on skills, job architecture, and career & talent development.

Let's get into it.

Part 1 — Job Architecture Finally Reflects Reality

For years, one of the most common blockers I have seen in real-world implementations has been the two-level job architecture in Job Profile Builder: Family → Role. It was a clean model on paper, but it did not survive contact with most large organisations. Engineering, Sales, Operations and Clinical functions rarely sit in a single flat family. They have sub-disciplines, specialisations, career streams, and technical ladders that matter enormously for skills mapping, learning and mobility.

Multi-Level Job Architecture in Job Profile Builder

This is, in my view, the headline feature of the entire 1H 2026 release on the skills side.

Customers can now configure up to four sub-family levels between Family and Role, giving a maximum of six levels in the hierarchy. Attributes — skills, competencies, and custom attributes — can be assigned at any level, and they cascade down through the hierarchy to the role, and from the role into the employee's Growth Portfolio and every downstream module that depends on the role (Performance, Career, Succession, Recruiting).

The nuance I want to highlight is that this cascading behaviour applies even if you are not using multiple levels yet. If you only have Family and Role today, you can now assign attributes at the family level and they will flow to the role and into the Growth Portfolio. That single change alone simplifies governance enormously for organisations with large, well-structured role catalogues.

At role level you retain full override flexibility — you can change proficiencies or replace inherited skills without affecting the parent level.

Strategic implication: If you have previously made painful architectural compromises to fit your organisation into the two-level model — flattening sub-disciplines, duplicating roles across families, or pushing complexity into custom fields — now is the time to revisit your design. Multi-level architecture is not just a technical upgrade; it is an opportunity to rebuild your job catalogue in a way that reflects how your business actually operates, and to do so without losing the inheritance benefits that make maintenance sustainable.

Self-Service Upgrade to JPB via Upgrade Center

Another quiet-but-important change: customers can now trigger the upgrade to Job Profile Builder directly from Admin Center → Upgrade Center, rather than raising a provisioning request through SAP Professional Services or an implementation partner.

This lowers the barrier to entry significantly. For customers who have been sitting on JDM simply because the upgrade felt bureaucratic, that excuse is now gone. (A friendly warning: if you see "Job Profile Builder" under Important Upgrades, it means you are only partially upgraded today — the old JDM-to-JPB technical sync job and the JPB enablement are now consolidated into a single action, and completing this is a prerequisite for the multi-level architecture feature.)

Migration of Role Descriptions from JDM to JPB

Historically, one of the real frictions in upgrading from JDM was that role descriptions did not migrate cleanly — customers had to export, re-format and re-import content into the new job profile structure. With 1H 2026, JDM role descriptions are automatically converted into job profiles in JPB during the upgrade. This removes a real practical barrier and should accelerate downstream adoption of Recruiting, Performance and Career processes that depend on JPB.

The Big One: JDM Retirement by 2H 2027

SAP has now formally announced the sunset of Job Description Manager by 2H 2027. This is not a surprise — development on JDM effectively stopped some years ago, and JPB has been the strategic successor for five-plus years — but the formal retirement date changes the conversation.

If you are still on JDM, you now have a clear, time-bound roadmap decision to make. My advice:

  • Treat this as a strategic opportunity, not a technical migration. Most organisations I speak with carry five to ten years of accumulated job-catalogue technical debt. Use the move to JPB as the forcing function to clean it up.
  • Plan for the Talent Intelligence Hub at the same time. JPB is a prerequisite for TIH, and TIH unlocks AI-driven experiences (skills inferencing, career intelligence, Joule agents) that will increasingly differentiate employee experience. Doing these sequentially often doubles the change-management effort; doing them together lets you tell one coherent story to the business.
  • Do not wait until 2027. The teams who wait will be competing for the same scarce SAP Professional Services and partner capacity in the final months before the cutoff.

JPB will remain the admin interface where you define families, roles and the organisational job structure. TIH is the repository for person-related attributes (skills, competencies, certificates). The two live side-by-side and depend on each other.

Part 2 — TIH Matures: Certificates, Governance, and Trust

The second major theme of 1H 2026 is that the Talent Intelligence Hub is growing up. Skills data is finally being treated with the same rigour we have always applied to core HR data — with proper sources of truth, governance, and access control.

Certificates as a First-Class Attribute in TIH

Certificate Administration arrives as a new attribute type in TIH, with a full lifecycle: administrators can define certificates, attach documents and credential metadata, set validity periods and renewal requirements, and — crucially — link certificates to skills and required proficiency levels.

From the employee side, adding a certificate to the Growth Portfolio automatically grants the associated skills at the configured proficiency levels. If the employee already holds the skill, the certificate appears in the skill's timeline. Expiry reminders are triggered automatically at 90/60/30/7 days ahead of expiry, and managers get a centralised Team View of certificate status across direct reports.

One thoughtful design choice worth flagging: when a certificate expires or is deleted, the associated skills are retained. This matches the existing Growth Portfolio behaviour (skills persist even if a role mapping changes) and avoids the pathological outcome of employees losing capability ratings overnight because a training record lapsed.

Where this matters most: regulated industries. Manufacturing, pharmaceutical, utilities, financial services, aviation, healthcare — any sector where compliance audits depend on demonstrating that staff hold current credentials — now has a native, skills-linked, auditable system of record inside SuccessFactors. This is a big deal. For my manufacturing and life-sciences clients, this single feature changes the ROI calculation on TIH.

A couple of items I would watch on the roadmap: native integration with LMS curricula and background elements, and the ability to associate certificates as role attributes in Job Profile Builder. SAP has confirmed both are in development but not yet delivered.

 

 

Skills Governance Goes Mainstream (with AI)

From 1H 2026, Skills Governance is enabled by default in all instances and sits within Manage Talent Intelligence Hub. If you have AI-assisted skills inferencing enabled, newly inferred skills flow into an Inferred tab where administrators can review, standardise, or reject them before they land in the official skills library. Imported skills can optionally be subjected to the same governance flow.

The enriched Admin UI consolidates attribute management, settings, tags, rating scales and rating history into a structured workspace — a significant improvement for skills architects who previously had to navigate across several screens.

 

My view: this is the single most important governance capability TIH has ever shipped. One of the consistent failure modes I have seen in skills programmes is the rapid proliferation of near-duplicate, inconsistently named skills from multiple sources (performance feedback, CVs, third-party inference engines, manual entry). Without governance, the skills library becomes noisy within months and trust collapses. AI-assisted standardisation, inside the suite, is how you keep the library clean at enterprise scale.

Manage Data Sources & Manage Growth Portfolio Ratings

These two features are twin pillars of the same idea: controlled, auditable, trusted skills data.

  • Manage Data Sources lets administrators register external skills providers (Beamery, Degreed, TechWolf, TalenTeam, and others) as verified sources feeding into TIH and the Growth Portfolio. You can assign a custom display name so that what appears in the employee's timeline matches how your organisation describes the process (e.g., "Career Pulse" rather than the vendor's product name).
  • Manage Growth Portfolio Ratings lets you decide, per internal and external source, whether that source is allowed to overwrite proficiency levels, or only to raise them. It is now possible to say: "Our Performance Management forms can overwrite proficiency levels, but our skills inference engine can only add signals and increase ratings — never lower them."

If you are currently integrating external skills data into Growth Portfolio via Inbound APIs, note that this is no longer optional: you must register your sources and assign the new Import/Export Talent Intelligence Hub and Growth Portfolio Data role-based permission, otherwise data flow will break. Plan this as part of your 1H 2026 release testing.

Restricting Attributes by Type or Tag

A governance feature customers have been asking for: you can now configure target criteria in RBP to restrict which attribute types (skills, competencies, custom) or tags a particular user or group can see and act on across Growth Portfolio, Opportunity Marketplace, People Profile, Job Profile Builder, Succession, Performance Management and more. It is optional, with specific supported and excluded scenarios documented — but for organisations juggling compliance constraints across geographies and business units, this is the granularity that has been missing.

Part 3 — Employee and Manager Experience

Skill-Based Search in People Search

With Growth Portfolio enabled, users can now search for employees by skills, attributes and proficiency levels directly from the enhanced People Search experience. For internal mobility, project staffing, and rapid expertise discovery, this is a long-overdue capability. You will need to manually enable Enhanced People Search in Admin Center and ensure the Skill and Attribute search criteria is selected — this is not automatic on upgrade.

Growth Portfolio on Mobile

Growth Portfolio is now available on the SuccessFactors mobile app (iOS and Android). This is a first-phase release focused on employee self-service — adding and managing attributes on the go. The web experience remains richer, but the mobile capability will materially change adoption curves, especially for frontline, field and deskless workforces. For my manufacturing and retail clients, this is the difference between a skills programme that reaches head office and one that reaches the whole business.

Growth Portfolio Data Export Enhancements

Finally — and this will make integration architects very happy — you can now filter and sort Growth Portfolio data in Integration Center using the new Filter and Sort tab, including by personUuid, libraryId, proficiency level, status and last modified date. Combined with the new dedicated import/export RBP, this gives much tighter control over what flows in and out of the skills repository.

Part 4 — Career & Talent Development: Where the Skills Story Pays Off

Everything in Parts 1 to 3 is foundation. Part 4 is where the foundation starts paying dividends for employees, managers, and talent leaders.

Show Upcoming Role for Internal Hires

When an employee has accepted an internal move, their upcoming role now appears in the Growth Portfolio alongside their current role — with role details, skill gaps, and development actions. This removes the awkward gap between offer acceptance and effective start date, where an internal hire has historically had no way to start preparing.

Enhanced Team Development Insights

Managers can now see, for each direct report: detailed role information, skills and proficiency levels, and the skills each team member has marked as a passion. This last point — surfacing skill passions at the team level — is small in engineering terms but large in coaching terms. It gives managers something more human to discuss than a compliance-driven development conversation.

Enhanced Personalised Recommendations

This is one of the quieter but more important upgrades. Recommendations for roles, open jobs, and learning are now materially smarter:

  • Roles: recommendations can now filter out roles below the employee's current level, using a number-type custom field. No more demotion-shaped career paths.
  • Learning: items linked to more relevant skills appear first, and items below the employee's current proficiency level are excluded. This raises the quality bar considerably.
  • Open Jobs: administrators can configure preferred locations and appropriate role levels as filter criteria. Mismatched jobs are simply not recommended rather than shown as noise.

Taken together, these changes move the recommendation engine from "broadly relevant" to "genuinely personalised" — which is what it takes for employees to actually trust the system enough to act on it.

Invite Users to Apply for Assignments

Assignment owners and co-owners can now proactively identify candidates and invite them to apply to Opportunity Marketplace assignments. Invited users receive an email, a home-page invitation card (if the latest home page is enabled), and see the assignment surfaced at the top of their Opportunity Marketplace and Growth Portfolio recommendations. This closes the loop on internal mobility — not just discovery, but active outreach.

Custom Attributes in Talent Search

Talent Search can now use custom attributes alongside skills and competencies, including side-by-side proficiency comparison and export of attribute data. For organisations that have invested in custom attributes to capture regulatory certifications, clearances, language fluency, or industry-specific capabilities, this unlocks a much richer internal talent discovery experience.

Part 5 — The Joule Agents for Career & Talent Development

1H 2026 introduces three Joule agents directly relevant to the skills and talent development agenda.

Career Development Agent

The Career Development Agent guides employees through their career journey conversationally inside Joule. It evaluates strengths, identifies viable next roles grounded in skills data, and — here is the part that matters — can automatically assemble a personalised development plan with monthly milestones, recommended learning, recommended mentors, and concrete activities. It blends traditional role progressions with skills-adjacent opportunities, which matters for organisations trying to move beyond linear career ladders.

Development Goal Creation Agent

This agent generates personalised, measurable development goals grounded in CPM feedback, role data, and skills insights. Instead of vague competency-based goals ("improve leadership"), it produces impact statements with timelines ("Enhance cost control and budget management skills to improve financial oversight — 6-month time frame"). It also surfaces gaps, duplicates, and missing metrics across existing goals — which, in practice, is probably where most of the real hygiene value sits.

Succession Planning Agent

For succession planners, this agent performs a succession health check — identifying critical positions with no named successors, positions with low bench strength, and organisation-wide risk patterns. It then provides prioritised action plans. For large enterprises with hundreds of critical positions, this compresses days of manual analysis into minutes.

A pragmatic reminder on licensing. Joule agents require a Joule Premium license plus AI Units for consumption-based pricing. This is something to scope and plan with your account team early — the business case for agents is strong, but it is not a free upgrade. Treat it the way you would any consumption-based cloud service: model your usage, set guardrails, and tie adoption to measurable value.

What Leaders Should Actually Do Now

Rather than end with a feature summary, let me end with the conversations I think you should have in the next quarter:

  1. Book the JDM-to-JPB conversation on your 2026 roadmap. The 2H 2027 sunset is non-negotiable, and the firms that plan early will migrate with more control, cleaner data, and less partner capacity risk.
  2. Revisit your job architecture. Multi-level capability changes what good looks like. If your current model was a compromise, now is the moment to correct it — and to let cascading attributes do work that job-code gymnastics used to do.
  3. Stand up skills governance now. Skills Governance is on by default from 1H 2026. Assign a skills data steward. Define your policy for inferred, imported and standardised skills. The library only stays trustworthy if someone owns it.
  4. Register your external skills sources and tighten your import/export permissions. If you are integrating Beamery, Degreed, TechWolf, TalenTeam or any other partner, you will need to register and configure permissions to keep data flowing. Plan this as part of release testing.
  5. Make certificates part of your compliance story. If you operate in a regulated industry, this is the release where SuccessFactors genuinely becomes a compliance system of record for workforce credentials. Build the business case around audit readiness, not just HR efficiency.
  6. Design your Joule agent rollout deliberately. Start with the agent that matches your highest-pain use case — Succession Planning Agent for large complex organisations, Career Development Agent for organisations trying to drive internal mobility, Development Goal Creation Agent for organisations with goal-quality issues. Measure before you scale.

Closing Thoughts

I have written before about mastering the implementation of SAP SuccessFactors Talent Intelligence Hub, about building the business case for skills, and about where organisations sit on the skills maturity curve. Across all of those pieces, one theme recurs: skills transformation is not a product implementation — it is a business transformation enabled by a product.

1H 2026 is the release where SAP SuccessFactors' product finally gives you most of what you need to run that transformation at scale. Multi-level architecture, skills governance, source-of-truth controls, certificates, mobile, consent-based succession, and the Career & Talent Development Joule agents together form a genuinely coherent platform.

The hard work — job architecture design, skills governance operating model, change management, adoption, business-case tracking — is still on you and your teams. But the tooling has caught up to the ambition.

If your organisation is starting this journey, or if you have hit a wall with an existing TIH or skills programme, I am always happy to compare notes. Reach out in the comments, or find us at talenteam.com.

And if you are joining us at our Learning & Skills Forum in Philadelphia, Chicago or London this year, this release — and what it means for the future of the skills-based organisation — will be front and centre.

entry.title: High-Availability Setup for SAP Secure Login Service with AWS Route 53
entry.content:
Introduction

Inspired by the initial blog by our Product team members

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/high-availability-setup-for-sap-secure-login-service-with-microsoft-azure/ba-p/14140475

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI enables secure authentication and single sign-on (SSO) for SAP GUI using SAP BTP, leveraging its built-in platform capabilities for high availability 

In most cases, this is sufficient, as the service is only used during the user’s initial login. Once authenticated, an X.509 certificate provides SSO for the rest of the session.

However, some organizations require higher resilience and opt for a multi-region failover setup. In this blog, I’ll walk through how to implement this using SAP Secure Login Service, SAP BTP Custom Domain Service, and AWS Route 53.

Architecture 

Prerequisites

SAP BTP

  • Two subaccounts in different regions (e.g. US10, US21, EU10 etc). For this blog i have used US10 and EU10.
  • SLS subscribed in both subaccounts
  • Custom Domain Service entitlement assigned and activated in both subaccounts

AWS

  • Route 53 hosted zone for your domain

Identity

  • IAS tenants configured with SLS

Certificates

  • TLS certificate for sls.yourdomain.com (from a public CA)

Step 1: Configure Custom Domain Service — US10 (Primary)

In the US10 subaccount, open Custom Domain Manager and complete the following:

Reserve domain: yourdomain.com

Create custom domain: sls.yourdomain.com

 

Create a SaaS route: map sls.yourdomain.com to the SLS subscription

 

Perform TLS configurations

Create a Server Identity and generate a CSR (Certificate Signing Request)

Obtain a TLS certificate from a public CA using the CSR and Upload the full certificate chain and activate it

Activate it

Note the CNAME target shown under "DNS CNAME Recommendation" — you will need this exact value for Route 53

About the CNAME target
The CNAME target provided by Custom Domain Manager is SAP's ingress hostname for SaaS routes in the US10 region. Use exactly what Custom Domain Manager shows. Do not substitute or guess any hostname.

Step 2: Configure Custom Domain Service — EU10 (Secondary)

Repeat exactly the same steps in the EU10 subaccount. Use the same custom domain: sls.yourdomain.com.

Note the CNAME target for EU10 as well.

Why the same hostname in both subaccounts?
Each subaccount independently maps sls.yourdomain.com to its local SLS SaaS instance. This is fully supported by Custom Domain Service. Both instances respond to the same hostname, which is what makes Route 53 failover work transparently. Route 53 resolves sls.yourdomain.com to the CNAME target of whichever region is active — and that region's SLS instance is already configured to serve requests for that hostname.

Step 3: Configure IAS Redirect URI

In your IAS tenants, add the following as an allowed redirect URI for the SLS application:

https://sls.yourdomain.com/login/callback

 

Keep all existing redirect URIs — just add this one. Because both SLS instances share the same hostname, this single redirect URI covers both regions regardless of which instance handles the authentication request.

 Each SLS instance uses a separate IAS tenant, add this redirect URI in both tenants ( US10 and EU10)

Step 4: Configure Route 53 Failover Routing

Health Checks

Create two Route 53 health checks — one per SLS instance — targeting each SLS instance hostname directly:

Health Check 1 (Primary — US10):

  • Protocol: HTTPS
  • Endpoint: <your-us10-sls-hostname> (the actual SLS instance hostname in US10, e.g. yourinstance.us10.sls.cloud.sap)
  • Path: /health
  • Port: 443
  • Purpose: Monitors US10 SLS instance directly

Health Check 2 (Secondary — EU10):

  • Protocol: HTTPS
  • Endpoint: <your-eu10-sls-hostname> (the actual SLS instance hostname in EU10, e.g. yourinstance.eu10.sls.cloud.sap)
  • Path: /health
  • Port: 443
  • Purpose: Monitors EU10 SLS instance directly

(like primary US10 setup)

Why target the SLS instance hostname directly?
The health checks must target each SLS instance hostname directly — not sls.yourdomain.com. At any point in time, sls.yourdomain.com only resolves to the currently active region. A health check on sls.yourdomain.com would only monitor whichever instance Route 53 is currently routing to, and would never detect that the primary is down (because Route 53 would have already switched). By monitoring each SLS instance hostname independently, Route 53 can detect failures in either region at any time.

CNAME records for failover

Primary Record

Name:            sls.yourdomain.com

Type:            CNAME

Value:           api.cf.us10.hana.ondemand.com

Routing policy:  Failover — PRIMARY

Health check:    Yes (associate Health Check 1 — US10)

 

 

Secondary Record

Similarly for Secondary

 

TTL recommendation
Could set TTL to 60 seconds. A lower TTL means faster DNS propagation during failover events.

Step 5: Configure SAP Secure Login Client

Configure SAP Secure Login Client (SLC) with the SLS URL pointing to your custom domain:

https://sls.yourdomain.com/slc/v1/login

 

 

Distribute this configuration via:

  • GPO (Group Policy Object)
  • Intune
  • SCCM

Because the URL always points to sls.yourdomain.com, no SLC reconfiguration is needed when a failover occurs. Route 53 changes the DNS resolution transparently.

Failover Behavior

Scenario

Result

Primary healthy

Traffic → US10 SLS SaaS instance

Primary fails health check

Route 53 switches DNS to EU10 CNAME target

Primary recovers

Route 53 switches traffic back to US10

Failover Timing

  • Health check detection: ~30–90 seconds
  • DNS TTL propagation: ~60 seconds
  • Total worst-case failover time: approximately 2 minutes
 

 

Important Considerations

  1. Failover Is NOT Session-Aware
    Active login flows that are in progress during a failover event may fail. Users may need to retry authentication. Design user communications accordingly and set expectations with support teams.
  2. SLS Instances Are Independent
    Both SLS instances must be kept in sync manually. This includes IAS configuration, authentication policies, certificate trust configuration, and version levels. Drift between instances can cause inconsistent behavior depending on which region is active.
  3. Certificate Strategy
    You can use the same TLS certificate (same Subject Alternative Name) across both regions for simplicity, since both Custom Domain Manager instances are serving the same hostname. Alternatively, use separate certificates per region for better security isolation. Either approach is valid.
  4. Health Checks on Both Records
    It is recommended to add health checks on both primary and secondary records. Without a health check on the secondary, Route 53 may route traffic to a failed secondary during a primary failover, resulting in an outage even though the primary was available.

Testing

1. DNS Verification

nslookup sls.yourdomain.com

Confirm it resolves to the US10 CNAME target under normal conditions.

2. End-to-End Login

Open SAP GUI Secure Login Client and authenticate end-to-end. Confirm the full OIDC flow completes, including the redirect back to sls.yourdomain.com/login/callback.

3. Failover Simulation

Disable the primary health check in Route 53. Wait approximately 2 minutes, then run:

nslookup sls.yourdomain.com

Confirm it now resolves to the EU10 CNAME target.

4. Secondary Authentication

Verify that authentication still works end-to-end when Route 53 is routing to the EU10 instance.

5. Failback

Re-enable the primary health check. After the TTL expires, confirm DNS resolves back to the US10 CNAME target and authentication continues to work.

SAP Backend user mapping

There are two options to ensure that the SNC mapping remains intact:

  • Use parameters of the SAP Cryptographic Library to strip the region names from the certificate subject before the mapping takes place. That way, certificates from different regions will match the same SNC name. See note 2338952 for details.
  • Change the SAP Secure Login Service configuration to use a customer-managed certificate authority. This gives you the option to customize the subject name of the certificate so that it does not include the BTP region.

Conclusion

By combining SAP BTP Custom Domain Service and AWS Route 53 failover routing, you eliminate the single point of failure in SAP GUI authentication. The key enabler is the shared custom domain: both SLS SaaS instances respond to sls.yourdomain.com, making the OIDC redirect_uri consistent across regions regardless of which instance is currently active.

Route 53 health checks detect failures automatically and update DNS within approximately two minutes. End users and SAP Secure Login Client configurations require no changes when a failover or failback occurs.

Final Takeaway
This setup provides:

  • High availability for SAP GUI authentication via a managed SaaS architecture
  • Transparent DNS-level failover with no client reconfiguration
  • A consistent OIDC redirect URI across both regions

 

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI @Christian_Cohrs1

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5: ISSUE : Calling Deep Entity of JSON - SAP UI...
entry.content:
You can try this, I am wondering if bindings like that for a v2 odata model is supported. For JSON model it is however.

<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstAddressCity' }" />
<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstEmail' }" />
<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstPhone' }" />

JS Formatter:

formatter: {
    firstAddressCity(oAddresses) {
        console.log(`Addresses`, oAddresses);
        return oAddresses?.results?.[0]?.CityName ?? "";
    },

    firstEmail(oAddresses) {
        const oFirstAddress = oAddresses?.results?.[0];
        return oFirstAddress?.to_EmailAddress?.results?.[0]?.EmailAddress ?? "";
    },

    firstPhone(oAddresses) {
        const oFirstAddress = oAddresses?.results?.[0];
        return oFirstAddress?.to_MobilePhoneNumber?.results?.[0]?.PhoneNumber ?? "";
    }
}

If it still doesn't work, there is one console log. Check it and you can see how the structure is.

entry.title: Introduction: Software Engineer Exploring SAP BTP & Enterprise Integration

Who I am

Hi I'm Myrr, based in ATL, GA, working as a software engineer focused on building scalable web products.

How I got here

I started my career in a completely different field (environmental chemistry), and transitioning into tech showed me how much I enjoy solving complex, open-ended problems. That curiosity is what led me to explore SAP and enterprise systems more deeply.

What I’m building right now

I’m currently working through SAP BTP-related learning and certifications while also building my own product on the side, with a focus on using data in meaningful ways.

My SAP interests

I’m especially interested in SAP BTP and how modern applications integrate with enterprise systems. I’m drawn to the intersection of backend architecture, APIs, and scalable product design.

I’d love to connect with:

  • SAP BTP developers and architects
  • People working on real-world enterprise integrations
  • Hiring managers or teams building modern SAP-based products
  • Anyone else navigating a transition into SAP or enterprise tech

Looking forward to learning, building, and connecting with all of you 😁

claude plugin install ui5@claude-plugins-official

entry.title: order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not zero?
entry.content:
Dear SAP ,

 

Why in app Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based, order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not zero?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not...
entry.content:
The Order Balance typically hits zero because every cost debited to the order is offset by a credit.

The Total Cost Variance is an analytical value that measures the difference between the Actual Costs incurred and the Target Costs (standard cost based on the quantity received).

Even if the balance is zero, the variance remains non-zero.

entry.title: Re: How to dynamically determine Warehouse Source Storage...
entry.content:
Hello,

The most standard way to achieve this requirement in EWM would be to have 3 different storage types mapped to 3 different availability group. This would mean having ECC setup which creates a sales delivery from the right storage location dependent on the customer. I doubt the ECC setup is standard and would need some coding. To complete the setup, you need to define 3 stock types and the stock removal search sequence dependent on the stock types.

Alternatively, you can meet this requirement in EWM directly by putting a custom code in the BADI which uses interface  /SCWM/IF_EX_CORE_RMS_STRATEGY. I would suggest careful due diligence is done considering future implications and data maintenance.

Regards,

Sushant

Regards

entry.title: Handling Cross-App Navigation from Flat to Hierarchical Models in Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Cross-app navigation in SAP Fiori Elements (CAP, OData V4) can become tricky when the source and target applications follow different data models. 

In our case, we had a scenario where a flat source application needed to navigate to a hierarchical target application using intent-based navigation.

• Source App: Flat entity model, exposing fields from multiple levels of Target App's hierarchy in a single entity. Navigation source: List Report
• Target App: Hierarchical entity model, top-to-bottom. Navigation target: Object Page

Goal: A row selection in the Source App's List Report triggers intent-based navigation to the corresponding Object Page in the Target App.

 

Standard Setup

  • Semantic Object annotation on Source App's entity
annotate service.SourceAppEntity with @(Common: {SemanticKey: [ID]});
  •  Outbound navigation in Source App's  manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "outbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
          "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
          "action": "manage"
        }
    }
}
  • Inbound navigation in TargetApp's manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "inbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
            "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
            "action": "manage",
            "signature": {       
              "parameters": {},
              "additionalParameters": "allowed"
            }
        }
     }
}

 

The Problem

With the above configuration in place, the navigation was triggered but failed to resolve correctly to the target Object Page. Inspecting the navigation call in the browser showed that the FLP was receiving the entire binding context of the selected row as navigation parameters.

Since the 'Source App' is a flat entity, this included:

  • Fields from multiple hierarchy levels
  • Fields not present in Target App's entity

The Launchpad attempts to resolve navigation using all provided parameters. When those parameters don’t align with the target entity, resolution becomes unreliable.

The semantic key (ID), the only required parameter was effectively buried among irrelevant fields.

 

Root Cause

This is a data model mismatch:

  • Source App is intentionally broad (flat) to surface data across levels
  • Target App is narrow (hierarchical) and expects a specific key

By default, Fiori Elements passes the full row context and does not account for this mismatch.

 

Solution: @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

The UI vocabulary provides an annotation specifically for this [1] : @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

Applied to a property, it tells the Fiori Elements framework to exclude that field from the navigation context payload when intent-based navigation is triggered.

Apply it to every field in Source App's entity except the semantic key:

annotate service.SourceAppEntity with {
    field1  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field2  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field3  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    .
    .
     // repeat for all fields except ID
};

With this in place, the navigation context carries only the ID, which matches the Target App’s inbound signature and allows the Object Page to resolve correctly

 

Key Takeaway

When navigating from a flat source model to a hierarchical target model, this leads to unnecessary parameters being included in the navigation payload. @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext allows explicit control so that only the semantic key is passed to the target application.

entry.title: KE28: Additional "Process" section is displayed in Technical Settings in R3M only
entry.content:
During the screen comparison between the 2023 system and the 2025 upgraded system, we found a layout difference on the Technical Settings screen in transaction KE28.

In R3M (2025), an additional section named "Process" is displayed at the bottom of the screen.
This section contains fields such as:
- Post Receivers with Amounts From
- Currency Type for Amount Check

However, this "Process" section is not displayed in R4M (2023).

This issue was identified during the upgrade comparison between the 2023 environment and the 2025 environment.
We would like to confirm whether this additional section is an expected standard change after the upgrade or an unintended issue.

Please also advise whether this difference is standard system behavior and whether any functional impact should be considered for KE28 processing.

entry.title: Re: missing cloud alert emails
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Please know such cloud service notifications need to be subscribed on customer side, either by user him/herself or by the super user or cloud admins.

Sorry that we SAP Support cannot assist with the subscription on behalf of customer.

If you would like to do the subscription with your own S-user, then pls kindly refer to the following KBA 2900069 and the get the required S-user authorizations (Display System Data or Edit System data):

You may also contact a Super or Cloud Administrator to manage your subscriptions as described in KBA 2896942. 

  • KBA 2900069 - How S-users manage their own Cloud Notification subscriptions - SAP for Me  
  • KBA 2896942 - How Super and Cloud Administrators manage Cloud Notification subscriptions for other users - SAP for Me 

Hope the above information helps. 

If further issue during the subscription, or you have done the subscription but you still have issue to get the notification emails as expected, feel free to let us know by creating a case using component XX-SER-FORME.

Thanks and best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: [S/4 HANA Public Cloud] eDocuments created for Peppol...
entry.content:
There are two options:

Option1 : 

Control via eDocument Type / Country Assignment (Configuration-Based)

SAP DRC determines eligibility before submission based on:

  • Company Code country
  • eDocument Type
  • Scenario / Network (PEPPOL)

What to do:

  1. Create / use country‑specific eDocument types

    • Example:
      • INV_PEPPOL_BE → Belgium
      • INV_PEPPOL_DE → Germany

  2. Assign eDocument types strictly by company code country

    • Belgium company code → BE eDocument type only
    • Germany company code → DE eDocument type only

  3. Do NOT assign PEPPOL eDocument type to cross‑border scenario

    • DE company code + BE customer → no valid PEPPOL eDocument type exists
      → eDocument is created but never submitted

Option 2:

BAdI-Based Filtering at Submission Level

If configuration alone is not enough (very common), SAP explicitly supports logic-based filtering using the eDocument framework.

Where to filter:

Using DRC technical enhancement points (country‑neutral, SAP‑recommended) [blog.sap-press.com]

What logic to apply:

Block submission when:

Company Code Country ≠ Customer Country

Example logic (conceptual):

  • DE company code
  • Customer country = BE
  • Channel = PEPPOL → Do not submit

My recommendation would be :

Primary control

  • Country‑specific PEPPOL eDocument types
  • Assigned strictly by company code country

Secondary (mandatory in real projects)

  • DRC BAdI filter:
    Allow PEPPOL only when Company Country = Customer Country
